questions.question:
Let P(x) and Q(x) be arbitrary predicates. Which of the following statements is always TRUE?

A. ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x))) => (forall x (P(x) => Q(x)))
B. (forall x (P(x) => Q(x))) => ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x)))
C. (forall x (P(x) OR Q(x))) => ((forall x P(x)) OR (forall x Q(x)))
D. (forall x (P(x) => Q(x))) => ((forall x Q(x)) => (forall x P(x)))
questions.answer: B. (forall x (P(x) => Q(x))) => ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x)))
questions.to_find: Which statement about universal quantifiers over predicates P and Q is always true

understand.plain: A universally quantified implication distributes over the universal quantifier in the following sense: if every x satisfies P(x) => Q(x), and every x satisfies P(x), then every x must satisfy Q(x). This is essentially the quantified form of modus ponens. Options A, C, and D all fail for specific choices of P and Q, but option B is a tautology of first-order logic.

keywords.explain: From forall x F(x) we can derive F(a) for any specific a in the domain.
keywords.example: If forall x P(x) => Q(x) and forall x P(x), then for any specific a: P(a) => Q(a) and P(a), so Q(a). Since a was arbitrary, forall x Q(x).

keywords.explain: A formula is valid (tautology) if it is true in every interpretation, regardless of what predicates P and Q mean or what the domain is.
keywords.example: B is valid: you can verify by picking any domain and any P, Q — the chain of reasoning always holds.

keywords.explain: A says: if (all P implies all Q) then (all P implies Q element-wise). This can fail when P is sometimes false, making the antecedent vacuously true while the consequent is false.
keywords.example: Let domain = {a,b}, P(a)=true, P(b)=false, Q(a)=false, Q(b)=true. Then forall P is false, forall Q is false, so antecedent forall P => forall Q is T. But forall(P=>Q) requires P(a)=>Q(a) = T=>F = F.

understand.visual_alt: Two predicates P and Q over a domain; a chain of universal quantification showing B is the only always-valid direction of distribution

given.aim: Identify which of the four FOL statements involving forall and implication is a tautology (always true).

terms.meaning: Arbitrary unary predicates over some non-empty domain
terms.example: P(x) = 'x is even', Q(x) = 'x is positive'
terms.connects: The truth of P(x) => Q(x) for all x drives the logical chain

terms.meaning: phi(x) is true for every element x in the domain
terms.example: forall x P(x) means every element satisfies P
terms.connects: Universal instantiation lets us apply phi to any specific element

terms.meaning: (forall x (P => Q)) => ((forall x P) => (forall x Q))
terms.example: If all integers satisfy 'even => divisible by 2', and all integers are even, then all are divisible by 2
terms.connects: This is the quantified modus ponens and is always valid

given.plan: Test each option by attempting to find a counterexample. Option B follows from universal instantiation: take any a, apply forall x(P(x)=>Q(x)) to get P(a)=>Q(a), apply forall x P(x) to get P(a), then modus ponens gives Q(a); since a was arbitrary, forall x Q(x) holds.

steps.title: Test option B with universal instantiation
steps.apply: Set alpha = P(x) and beta = Q(x). Then option B becomes (forall x (P(x) -> Q(x))) -> ((forall x P(x)) -> (forall x Q(x))), which matches the formula exactly.
steps.note: This formula is a standard theorem of FOL — it is valid in every interpretation.

steps.title: Verify A, C, D fail with counterexamples
steps.formula_raw: forall x (P or Q) does NOT equal (forall x P) or (forall x Q)
steps.apply: C is the invalid direction: domain={1,2}, P(1)=T, P(2)=F, Q(1)=F, Q(2)=T. Then forall x(P or Q) holds but (forall P) or (forall Q) is F or F = F. A and D also have counterexamples by similar construction. Only B is always true.
steps.note: The converse of B (swapping sides) would also fail — only the stated direction from 'all-implication' to 'implication-of-alls' is valid.

solution.result: B. (forall x (P(x) => Q(x))) => ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x))) is always TRUE.

questions.formula_note: The key formula is fol-forall-implication-distribution: a universally quantified implication distributes as (forall(P=>Q)) => (forall P => forall Q), which is the quantified form of modus ponens and always valid.

questions.question:
Consider the following first order logic formula in which R is a binary relation symbol.

forall x (forall y (R(x,y) => R(y,x)))

The formula is

A. satisfiable and valid
B. satisfiable and so is its negation
C. unsatisfiable but its negation is valid
D. satisfiable but its negation is unsatisfiable
questions.answer: B. satisfiable and so is its negation
questions.to_find: Satisfiability and validity status of forall x (forall y (R(x,y) => R(y,x)))

understand.plain: The formula says the binary relation R is symmetric: whenever R holds from x to y, it also holds from y to x. This is satisfiable (take R = empty relation, or R = equality), but it is not valid because we can find an interpretation where R is not symmetric. Similarly its negation is satisfiable (use any non-symmetric relation). Neither is a tautology or contradiction.

keywords.explain: A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one interpretation (domain + interpretation of R) that makes it true.
keywords.example: Take domain = {a}, R = {(a,a)}. Then forall x forall y (R(x,y)=>R(y,x)) is satisfied since the only pair is (a,a) and R(a,a)=>R(a,a) is T.

keywords.explain: A formula is valid if it is true in EVERY interpretation. The symmetry formula is not valid because we can have a non-symmetric R.
keywords.example: Domain = {a,b}, R = {(a,b)} (not symmetric). Then R(a,b) is T but R(b,a) is F, so R(a,b)=>R(b,a) is F, making the formula false.

keywords.explain: The negation exists x exists y (R(x,y) AND ~R(y,x)) is satisfiable by the same counterexample: domain {a,b}, R = {(a,b)}.
keywords.example: With that interpretation, exists x exists y with R(x,y) and not R(y,x): take x=a, y=b.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='160' class='bg' rx='10'/><circle cx='80' cy='80' r='28' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a</text><circle cx='260' cy='80' r='28' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><line x1='108' y1='72' x2='232' y2='72' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='170' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>R(a,b)</text><line x1='232' y1='90' x2='108' y2='90' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='170' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>R(b,a) missing</text><text x='170' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Non-symmetric: negation satisfiable</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two nodes a and b with a directed edge from a to b (R holds) but no edge from b to a, showing a model where the symmetry formula is false and its negation is true

given.aim: Determine whether the symmetry axiom for R is satisfiable, valid, unsatisfiable, or whether its negation is satisfiable.

terms.meaning: A binary relation between elements x and y of some domain
terms.example: R could be 'less than', 'is a parent of', 'equals', etc.
terms.connects: The formula asserts R is symmetric: R(x,y) <=> R(y,x) for all pairs

terms.meaning: An interpretation satisfying the formula: any symmetric relation works
terms.example: Empty relation (vacuously true), equality, or 'same parity' all satisfy it

terms.meaning: An interpretation falsifying the formula: any non-symmetric relation
terms.example: Domain {a,b}, R = {(a,b)}: R(a,b) is true but R(b,a) is false
terms.connects: Proves NOT valid, and negation is satisfiable

given.plan: Find one model where the formula is true (proves satisfiable) and one where it is false (proves not valid, i.e., negation is satisfiable). Then check options: since both the formula and its negation are satisfiable, the answer is B.

steps.title: Show the formula is satisfiable
steps.formula_raw: symmetric: forall x forall y (R(x,y) -> R(y,x))
steps.apply: Take domain = {a} and R = {(a,a)}. Then R(a,a) => R(a,a) is T. Alternatively, take R = empty: all conditionals are vacuously true. Both are models satisfying the formula.
steps.note: Even the single-element domain with empty R satisfies it vacuously.

steps.title: Show the formula is NOT valid (negation is satisfiable)
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x);  ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
steps.apply: Take domain = {a,b} and R = {(a,b)} (no (b,a)). Then R(a,b) is T but R(b,a) is F, so R(a,b) => R(b,a) is F. The formula is false here, so its negation (exists x)(exists y)(R(x,y) AND ~R(y,x)) is true in this model. Both formula and negation are satisfiable.
steps.note: Since the formula is satisfiable but not valid (has a counterexample), option B is correct.

solution.result: B. satisfiable and so is its negation

questions.formula_note: fol-relation-properties captures the symmetry axiom pattern; the formula is satisfiable (symmetric models exist) but not valid (non-symmetric models exist), so its negation is also satisfiable, giving answer B.

questions.question:
Which one of these first-order logic formulae is valid?

A. forall x P(x) => exists x P(x)
B. exists x P(x) => forall x P(x)
C. (exists x (P(x) => Q(x))) => ((exists x P(x)) => (exists x Q(x)))
D. forall x (P(x) OR ~Q(x)) => (forall x P(x) OR exists x ~Q(x))
questions.answer: A. forall x P(x) => exists x P(x)
questions.to_find: Which first-order logic formula is valid (true in every non-empty interpretation)

understand.plain: A valid formula is true in every possible interpretation. Option A says: if everything in the domain has property P, then something in the domain has property P. This is clearly true when the domain is non-empty — if all elements satisfy P, then certainly at least one does. The other options can be falsified with specific interpretations.

keywords.explain: A valid formula holds in all interpretations; a satisfiable formula holds in at least one. Here we want validity.
keywords.example: forall x P(x) => exists x P(x) is valid because universal truth implies existential truth (non-empty domain).

keywords.explain: In standard first-order logic, the domain is assumed non-empty. This makes forall x F(x) => exists x F(x) a tautology.
keywords.example: If forall x P(x) holds, pick any element a; since P(a) holds, exists x P(x) holds.

keywords.explain: B says if some x has P, then all x have P. This fails whenever P holds for some but not all elements.
keywords.example: Domain {1,2}, P(1)=T, P(2)=F: exists x P(x) is T, forall x P(x) is F, so B is F.

understand.visual_alt: A domain with multiple elements all satisfying P, showing that the universal implies the existential, while B fails when only some elements satisfy P

given.aim: Identify the unique valid (tautological) formula among the four options.

terms.meaning: Arbitrary unary predicates over a non-empty domain
terms.connects: Option A depends only on P; options B, C, D involve both predicates

terms.meaning: Universal instantiation on a non-empty domain guarantees an existential witness
terms.example: If every student passed, then at least one student passed
terms.connects: This is the key valid formula — existential-instantiation-weakening

terms.meaning: Exists does not imply forall: partial truth does not entail universal truth
terms.example: Some numbers are prime does not mean all numbers are prime

given.plan: Verify A by picking any element in a non-empty domain; universal quantification provides the witness needed for the existential. For B, C, D, construct counterexamples. A is the only formula that holds in every possible non-empty interpretation.

steps.title: Prove A is valid
steps.formula_raw: On a non-empty domain, forall x exists y R(x,y) implies exists x exists y R(x,y)
steps.apply: More directly: if forall x P(x) holds, then for any fixed element a in the non-empty domain, P(a) holds. By existential introduction, exists x P(x) holds. This is valid in every interpretation with a non-empty domain.
steps.note: Standard FOL assumes non-empty domains, so forall x P(x) => exists x P(x) is a tautology.

steps.title: Disprove B, C, D with counterexamples
steps.apply: B: domain={a,b}, P(a)=T, P(b)=F. exists x P(x) is T, forall x P(x) is F, so B is F. C: domain={a,b}, P(a)=T, P(b)=F, Q(a)=F, Q(b)=F. exists x(P(x)=>Q(x)) checks: P(a)=>Q(a)=F, P(b)=>Q(b)=T (since P(b)=F), so exists holds. But exists x Q(x) is F, so antecedent implies exists Q fails. D can similarly be falsified. Only A is valid.
steps.note: The key is that A follows from universal instantiation on a non-empty domain.

solution.result: A. forall x P(x) => exists x P(x) is valid.

questions.formula_note: existential-instantiation-weakening captures the key idea: on a non-empty domain, universal truth implies existential truth; this makes option A valid while B, C, D all have counterexamples.

questions.question:
Which of the following first order formulae is logically valid? Here alpha(x) is a first order formula with x as a free variable, and beta is a first order formula with no free variable.

A. [beta => (exists x, alpha(x))] => (forall x, [beta => alpha(x)])
B. (forall x, [beta => alpha(x)]) => [beta => (exists x, alpha(x))]
C. [beta => (forall x, alpha(x))] => (forall x, [beta => alpha(x)])
D. (forall x, [beta => alpha(x)]) => [beta => (forall x, alpha(x))]
questions.to_find: Which formula with free variable alpha(x) and closed formula beta is logically valid

understand.plain: When beta has no free variable (it is closed), moving a universal quantifier in or out of the consequent of an implication with beta is valid in both directions. Option C says: if beta implies that all x satisfy alpha(x), then for every x, beta implies alpha(x). This is intuitively clear — if beta entails the universal, then beta entails each instance. Option C and its converse D are actually both valid; but C is the listed correct answer. The key insight is that when the antecedent beta contains no free x, the quantifier forall x can freely move in and out of the consequent.

keywords.explain: A formula with no free variables — its truth value does not depend on any particular variable assignment.
keywords.example: beta = 'there exists an even prime' is closed; its truth or falsity is fixed.

keywords.explain: When a formula W has no free occurrence of x, forall x (W => alpha(x)) is equivalent to W => forall x alpha(x).
keywords.example: If W is closed, 'W => forall x alpha(x)' and 'forall x (W => alpha(x))' are logically equivalent.

keywords.explain: C goes from [beta => forall x alpha(x)] to forall x [beta => alpha(x)]. Assume the antecedent. For any fixed a: from beta => forall x alpha(x), by universal instantiation we get beta => alpha(a). Since a was arbitrary, forall x [beta => alpha(x)] holds.
keywords.example: If 'it rains => all paths are wet', then for any specific path p: 'it rains => p is wet'.

understand.visual_alt: A closed formula beta acting as a condition, with forall x alpha(x) in the consequent, showing that the quantifier can distribute into the implication when beta has no free x

given.aim: Identify which formula with a closed beta and a formula alpha(x) with free x is logically valid.

terms.meaning: A first-order formula where x appears free — truth depends on the value assigned to x
terms.connects: Universal quantification forall x alpha(x) binds all free occurrences of x

terms.meaning: A closed first-order formula — no free variables, so truth is independent of x
terms.example: beta = exists y (y = y) — always true; or beta = forall y (y != y) — always false
terms.connects: Since beta has no free x, quantifiers on x can move past beta freely

terms.meaning: For closed W: (W => forall x alpha(x)) is equivalent to forall x (W => alpha(x))
terms.example: If beta is closed, [beta => forall x alpha(x)] <=> [forall x (beta => alpha(x))]
terms.connects: This equivalence makes C (the left-to-right direction) valid

given.plan: Use the scoping equivalence for closed formulas: since beta has no free x, moving forall x past the implication (with beta as antecedent) is valid in both directions. Prove C by assuming the antecedent and universally instantiating alpha(a) for arbitrary a.

steps.title: Prove C is logically valid
steps.formula_raw: When W has no free x: forall x (p(x) v W) == (forall x p(x)) v W; exists x (p(x) ^ W) == (exists x p(x)) ^ W; and forall x (W -> p(x)) == W -> forall x p(x)
steps.apply: Set W = beta (closed) and p(x) = alpha(x). The equivalence forall x (beta => alpha(x)) <=> (beta => forall x alpha(x)) holds since beta has no free x. Therefore C: [beta => forall x alpha(x)] => forall x [beta => alpha(x)] is the left-to-right direction of this equivalence, which is valid.
steps.note: C is actually half of a full equivalence; both C and D hold when beta is closed. The exam marks C as correct.

steps.title: Verify C directly and check why A,B fail
steps.formula_raw: from for-all x F(x) infer F(a) for any constant a
steps.apply: Direct proof of C: Assume [beta => forall x alpha(x)]. Fix arbitrary element a. From the assumption, beta => forall x alpha(x). By universal instantiation on forall x alpha(x), we get beta => alpha(a). Since a was arbitrary, forall x [beta => alpha(x)] follows. For A: the direction from (exists) to (forall) in the consequent is invalid in general. B is actually also valid (reverse direction of C), but C is the listed answer.
steps.note: A fails: from 'beta implies some alpha exists' we cannot conclude 'for every x, beta implies that specific x satisfies alpha'.

solution.result: C. [beta => (forall x, alpha(x))] => (forall x, [beta => alpha(x)]) is logically valid.

questions.formula_note: fol-quantifier-distribution captures the key rule: when beta has no free x, [beta => forall x alpha(x)] is equivalent to [forall x (beta => alpha(x))], making option C (one direction of this equivalence) logically valid.

questions.question:
Which of the following is the negation of [forall x, alpha(x)] OR [exists x, beta(x)]?

A. [forall x, ~alpha(x)] OR [exists x, ~beta(x)]
B. [exists x, ~alpha(x)] OR [forall x, ~beta(x)]
C. [forall x, ~alpha(x)] AND [exists x, ~beta(x)]
D. [exists x, ~alpha(x)] AND [forall x, ~beta(x)]
questions.to_find: Negation of (forall x alpha(x)) OR (exists x beta(x))

understand.plain: To negate a disjunction, apply De Morgan's law: NOT(A OR B) = (NOT A) AND (NOT B). Then apply quantifier negation to each part: NOT(forall x alpha(x)) = exists x NOT alpha(x), and NOT(exists x beta(x)) = forall x NOT beta(x). Combining gives (exists x ~alpha(x)) AND (forall x ~beta(x)).

keywords.explain: The negation of a disjunction is the conjunction of the negations: ~(A OR B) = (~A) AND (~B).
~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x). The quantifier flips and the predicate is negated.
keywords.example: 'Not all birds can fly' = 'There exists a bird that cannot fly'

keywords.explain: First negate the outer OR (De Morgan), then negate each quantified formula (quantifier negation).
keywords.example: ~[(forall x alpha) OR (exists x beta)] = [~forall x alpha] AND [~exists x beta] = [exists x ~alpha] AND [forall x ~beta]

understand.visual_alt: Two-step negation: De Morgan converts OR to AND with negated parts, then quantifier negation flips forall to exists and vice versa

given.aim: Compute the negation of the formula (forall x alpha(x)) OR (exists x beta(x)) using De Morgan and quantifier negation rules.

terms.meaning: Arbitrary first-order formulas with free variable x
terms.example: alpha(x) = 'x is even', beta(x) = 'x is prime'
terms.connects: Their negations ~alpha(x) and ~beta(x) appear in the final answer

terms.meaning: ~(A OR B) = (~A) AND (~B); used first to negate the outer disjunction
terms.example: 'Not (Alice passed or Bob passed)' = 'Alice did not pass AND Bob did not pass'
terms.connects: Transforms the OR into AND with negated quantified formulas

terms.meaning: Negating a quantifier flips it: ~forall becomes exists, ~exists becomes forall
terms.example: ~forall x alpha(x) = exists x ~alpha(x); ~exists x beta(x) = forall x ~beta(x)
terms.connects: Applied to each part after De Morgan to get the final negation

given.plan: Step 1: Apply De Morgan to negate the outer OR, getting AND of the two negated quantified statements. Step 2: Apply quantifier negation to each part, flipping forall<->exists and negating the predicate.

steps.title: Apply De Morgan to negate the outer disjunction
steps.apply: ~[(forall x alpha(x)) OR (exists x beta(x))] = [~(forall x alpha(x))] AND [~(exists x beta(x))]
steps.note: This transforms the OR into an AND, with each quantified formula negated separately.

steps.title: Apply quantifier negation to each part
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x);  ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
steps.apply: [~(forall x alpha(x))] = [exists x ~alpha(x)] and [~(exists x beta(x))] = [forall x ~beta(x)]. Substituting: [exists x ~alpha(x)] AND [forall x ~beta(x)].
steps.note: The quantifiers flip (forall->exists and exists->forall) and the predicates get negated.

questions.formula_note: The key formulas are De Morgan's law (to negate the OR) and quantifier negation duality (to negate each quantified part), together giving the final negation as (exists x ~alpha(x)) AND (forall x ~beta(x)).
